name: Egypt
overview: Egypt's Personal Data Protection Law (Law No. 151 of 2020) is a comprehensive GDPR-influenced statute, in force since October 2020. However, its Executive Regulations and the Personal Data Protection Centre — the licensing and enforcement body the law requires — have been delayed for years, so practical enforcement remains limited. The law notably requires licences/permits for processing sensitive data and for cross-border transfers.

regulator.name: Personal Data Protection Centre (establishment pending; oversight via Ministry of Communications and Information Technology)

legal_instruments.title: Law No. 151 of 2020 on the Protection of Personal Data
legal_instruments.issuing_authority: House of Representatives / President of Egypt
legal_instruments.summary: Comprehensive law: consent-based processing, controller/processor licensing regime, DPO requirement, breach notification (72 hours to the Centre), sensitive-data permits, cross-border transfer permits, and criminal penalties up to EGP 5 million.

developments.title: Executive Regulations remain pending
developments.summary: More than four years after the law's entry into force, the Executive Regulations and the Personal Data Protection Centre had still not been issued/established, deferring licensing and enforcement mechanics.

developments.title: PDPL enters into force
developments.summary: Law 151/2020 took effect with a compliance window tied to the (still-pending) Executive Regulations.

penalties: Fines from EGP 100,000 up to EGP 5 million and imprisonment for offences including unlawful sensitive-data processing and cross-border transfer without permit.
cross_border: Transfers require destination protection no less than the law's and a licence/permit from the Centre — an unusually strict permit-based model (mechanics await Executive Regulations).
